title: Two Sum
problem_summary: Find two indices in an array whose values sum to a target.
recall_trigger: Need a pair summing to target → store complements in a hashmap as you go.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every pair of elements.
approaches.key_observation: Nested loops cover all pairs but waste time re-scanning.
approaches.trade_off: O(n²) is too slow for large inputs.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: A hashmap lets you check 'have I seen the complement?' in O(1).
approaches.key_observation: For each num, target - num is the complement. Store seen values in a map.
approaches.trade_off: You must see every element at least once, so O(n) is optimal.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
problem_summary: Find the maximum profit from buying and selling a stock once.
recall_trigger: Track running minimum price → max profit is current - min so far.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Try every buy-sell pair.
approaches.key_observation: Check all i < j pairs for max prices[j] - prices[i].
approaches.trade_off: Quadratic — redundant comparisons.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Track the minimum price seen so far; at each step, the best sell is current price - min.
approaches.key_observation: One pass: maintain min_price and max_profit simultaneously.
approaches.code_hint:
min_price = inf
for price in prices:
  min_price = min(min_price, price)
  profit = max(profit, price - min_price)
approaches.trade_off: Can't do better than O(n) since every price must be examined.

similar_problems: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II

title: Contains Duplicate
problem_summary: Determine if any value appears at least twice in an array.
recall_trigger: Seen set → if already in set, duplicate found.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Compare every pair.
approaches.key_observation: Nested loop checks all pairs for equality.
approaches.trade_off: Too slow for large arrays.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: A set tracks seen values — duplicate means the value is already in the set.
approaches.key_observation: Set lookup is O(1). If len(set(nums)) < len(nums), duplicates exist.
approaches.code_hint: return len(nums) != len(set(nums))
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time is optimal; space trade-off is unavoidable without sorting.

title: Product of Array Except Self
problem_summary: Return an array where each element is the product of all other elements, without division.
recall_trigger: Prefix product left-to-right, then suffix product right-to-left.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: For each index, multiply all other elements.
approaches.key_observation: Nested loop computes product skipping current index.
approaches.trade_off: Quadratic — recomputes overlapping products.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Product except self = (product of everything to the left) × (product of everything to the right).
approaches.key_observation: Two passes: build prefix products L→R, then multiply suffix products R→L into the result.
approaches.code_hint:
result = [1]*n
prefix = 1
for i in range(n):
  result[i] = prefix
  prefix *= nums[i]
suffix = 1
for i in range(n-1,-1,-1):
  result[i] *= suffix
  suffix *= nums[i]
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time and O(1) extra space (output array doesn't count). Can't improve further.

title: Maximum Subarray
problem_summary: Find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum.
recall_trigger: Kadane's: extend current subarray or start fresh — max(num, current_sum + num).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Try all subarrays, track the maximum sum.
approaches.key_observation: Two nested loops enumerate all start/end pairs.
approaches.trade_off: Doesn't exploit the observation that negative prefixes should be discarded.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: At each position, either extend the previous subarray or start a new one.
approaches.key_observation: Kadane's algorithm: if current_sum < 0, reset to 0 (start fresh).
approaches.code_hint:
curr = best = nums[0]
for num in nums[1:]:
  curr = max(num, curr + num)
  best = max(best, curr)
approaches.trade_off: Linear scan is optimal — every element must be examined.

title: Maximum Product Subarray
problem_summary: Find the contiguous subarray with the largest product.
recall_trigger: Track both max AND min product — a negative × negative flips to max.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Compute product of every subarray.
approaches.key_observation: Nested loops with running product.
approaches.trade_off: Doesn't exploit that we only need running max and min.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: A negative number can turn a minimum product into the maximum. Track both.
approaches.key_observation: At each step: new_max = max(num, max_so_far*num, min_so_far*num). Swap when num is negative.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) is optimal. The min/max tracking handles all sign-flip cases.

title: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
problem_summary: Find the minimum element in a sorted array that has been rotated.
recall_trigger: Binary search — if mid > right, min is in right half; else left half.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Scan the entire array for the minimum.
approaches.key_observation: Linear scan always works but ignores the sorted structure.
approaches.code_hint: return min(nums)
approaches.trade_off: Doesn't exploit the sorted + rotated property.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: The rotation creates two sorted halves. Binary search finds the pivot.
approaches.key_observation: If nums[mid] > nums[right], the min must be in the right half (the rotation point is there).
approaches.code_hint:
lo, hi = 0, len(nums)-1
while lo < hi:
  mid = (lo+hi)//2
  if nums[mid] > nums[hi]: lo = mid+1
  else: hi = mid
return nums[lo]
approaches.trade_off: O(log n) is optimal for a search problem with sorted structure.

title: Search in Rotated Sorted Array
problem_summary: Search for a target in a rotated sorted array. Return index or -1.
recall_trigger: Determine which half is sorted → check if target is in that half.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Linear scan.
approaches.key_observation: Check every element.
approaches.trade_off: Ignores sorted structure.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: At least one half of the array is always sorted. Determine which, then check if target falls in that range.
approaches.key_observation: If left half sorted (nums[lo] <= nums[mid]), check if target is in [lo, mid]. Otherwise search right half.
approaches.code_hint:
lo, hi = 0, len(nums)-1
while lo <= hi:
  mid = (lo+hi)//2
  if nums[mid] == target: return mid
  if nums[lo] <= nums[mid]:  # left sorted
    if nums[lo] <= target < nums[mid]: hi = mid-1
    else: lo = mid+1
  else:  # right sorted
    if nums[mid] < target <= nums[hi]: lo = mid+1
    else: hi = mid-1
approaches.trade_off: O(log n) is optimal; leverages the partial sorted structure fully.

similar_problems: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
Search in Rotated Sorted Array II

title: 3Sum
problem_summary: Find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero.
recall_trigger: Sort → fix one number → two-pointer the rest. Skip duplicates.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check all triplets.
approaches.key_observation: Three nested loops, use a set to avoid duplicates.
approaches.trade_off: Cubic — far too slow for n > 1000.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sort the array. Fix one element, then use two pointers for the remaining two.
approaches.key_observation: Sorting enables two-pointer approach and easy duplicate skipping.
approaches.code_hint:
sort(nums)
for i in range(n):
  if i>0 and nums[i]==nums[i-1]: continue
  lo, hi = i+1, n-1
  while lo < hi:
    s = nums[i]+nums[lo]+nums[hi]
    if s < 0: lo++
    elif s > 0: hi--
    else: add, skip dupes
approaches.trade_off: O(n²) is optimal — can't avoid fixing one element and scanning the rest.

title: Container With Most Water
problem_summary: Find two lines that together with the x-axis form a container holding the most water.
recall_trigger: Two pointers at edges → move the shorter line inward (can only improve).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check all pairs of lines.
approaches.key_observation: Area = min(h[i], h[j]) × (j - i). Try all pairs.
approaches.trade_off: Doesn't exploit that moving the shorter line is the only way to potentially increase area.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Start with widest container. Moving the shorter pointer inward may find a taller line.
approaches.key_observation: Moving the taller pointer can only decrease width without guaranteed height increase. Always move the shorter one.
approaches.code_hint:
lo, hi = 0, n-1
while lo < hi:
  area = min(h[lo],h[hi])*(hi-lo)
  best = max(best, area)
  if h[lo] < h[hi]: lo++
  else: hi--
approaches.trade_off: O(n) is optimal. The greedy pointer movement covers all meaningful pairs.

title: Valid Anagram
problem_summary: Determine if two strings are anagrams (same characters, same frequency).
recall_trigger: Character frequency count → both strings must have identical counts.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Sort both strings and compare.
approaches.key_observation: Anagrams, when sorted, produce identical strings.
approaches.code_hint: return sorted(s) == sorted(t)
approaches.trade_off: Sorting is unnecessary overhead.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Count character frequencies. Anagrams have identical frequency maps.
approaches.key_observation: Use a single counter array of size 26. Increment for s, decrement for t.
approaches.code_hint:
count = [0]*26
for c in s: count[ord(c)-ord('a')] += 1
for c in t: count[ord(c)-ord('a')] -= 1
return all(x==0 for x in count)
approaches.trade_off: O(n) is optimal. Fixed 26-char space is effectively O(1).

title: Group Anagrams
problem_summary: Group strings that are anagrams of each other.
recall_trigger: Sorted string as hashmap key → all anagrams map to the same key.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Compare every pair of strings to check if they're anagrams.
approaches.key_observation: O(n²) pair comparisons, each taking O(k) for string length k.
approaches.code_hint: for each pair, check if sorted versions match
approaches.trade_off: Quadratic in number of strings.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: All anagrams produce the same sorted string. Use it as a hash key.
approaches.key_observation: sorted(str) or tuple(char_count) as dictionary key groups anagrams automatically.
approaches.trade_off: O(nk log k) from sorting each string. Could use counting sort for O(nk) but rarely needed.

similar_problems: Find All Anagrams in a String

title: Top K Frequent Elements
problem_summary: Return the k most frequent elements in an array.
recall_trigger: Count frequencies → bucket sort by frequency (or min-heap of size k).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Count frequencies, sort by frequency, take top k.
approaches.key_observation: Sorting all n elements by frequency and slicing.
approaches.code_hint:
count = Counter(nums)
return [x for x,_ in count.most_common(k)]
approaches.trade_off: Sorting is O(n log n) — overkill when we only need top k.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Bucket sort: index = frequency, value = list of elements with that frequency.
approaches.key_observation: Max frequency is n, so create n+1 buckets. Scan from highest bucket to get top k.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time. Bucket sort avoids comparison-based sorting entirely.

edge_cases: k equals number of unique elements

title: Encode and Decode Strings
problem_summary: Design an algorithm to encode a list of strings into a single string, and decode it back.
recall_trigger: Length-prefix encoding: '4#word' format → unambiguous delimiter.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Prefix each string with its length and a delimiter. No ambiguity possible.
approaches.key_observation: Format: len(s) + '#' + s. The length tells the decoder exactly how many chars to read.
approaches.code_hint:
encode: return ''.join(f'{len(s)}#{s}' for s in strs)
decode:
  i = 0; result = []
  while i < len(s):
    j = s.index('#', i)
    length = int(s[i:j])
    result.append(s[j+1:j+1+length])
    i = j+1+length
approaches.trade_off: O(n) is optimal — must read every character at least once.

title: Longest Consecutive Sequence
problem_summary: Find the length of the longest consecutive elements sequence in an unsorted array.
recall_trigger: HashSet → only start counting from sequence starts (num-1 not in set).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Sort the array, then scan for consecutive runs.
approaches.key_observation: After sorting, consecutive elements are adjacent.
approaches.code_hint:
sort(nums)
longest = curr = 1
for i in range(1, n):
  if nums[i]==nums[i-1]+1: curr++
  elif nums[i]!=nums[i-1]: curr=1
  longest = max(longest, curr)
approaches.trade_off: Sorting takes O(n log n). Can we do O(n)?

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Put all numbers in a set. For each number that starts a sequence (num-1 not in set), count how far it extends.
approaches.key_observation: Only start counting from the beginning of a sequence. This ensures each element is visited at most twice.
approaches.code_hint:
num_set = set(nums)
for num in num_set:
  if num-1 not in num_set:  # start of sequence
    length = 1
    while num+length in num_set: length++
    longest = max(longest, length)
approaches.trade_off: O(n) amortized — each element is part of exactly one sequence traversal.

title: Valid Palindrome
problem_summary: Check if a string is a palindrome, considering only alphanumeric characters and ignoring case.
recall_trigger: Two pointers from edges → skip non-alphanumeric → compare lowercase.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Filter the string, then compare with its reverse.
approaches.key_observation: Clean string then check s == s[::-1].
approaches.code_hint:
cleaned = ''.join(c.lower() for c in s if c.isalnum())
return cleaned == cleaned[::-1]
approaches.trade_off: Creates a new string — O(n) extra space.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Two pointers compare characters in-place, skipping non-alphanumeric.
approaches.key_observation: No extra string needed. Move pointers inward, skip invalid chars, compare.
approaches.code_hint:
lo, hi = 0, len(s)-1
while lo < hi:
  while lo<hi and not s[lo].isalnum(): lo++
  while lo<hi and not s[hi].isalnum(): hi--
  if s[lo].lower() != s[hi].lower(): return False
  lo++; hi--
approaches.trade_off: O(1) space, O(n) time — optimal on both.

title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problem_summary: Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.
recall_trigger: Sliding window with a set → expand right, shrink left when duplicate found.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every substring for uniqueness.
approaches.key_observation: Enumerate all substrings and check each for duplicates.
approaches.trade_off: Cubic — recomputes uniqueness from scratch each time.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sliding window: maintain a set of characters in the current window. Expand right, shrink left on duplicates.
approaches.key_observation: Use a hashmap to store the last index of each char. Jump the left pointer past the duplicate.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) — each character is processed at most twice (once by each pointer).

title: Longest Repeating Character Replacement
problem_summary: Find the longest substring where you can replace at most k characters to make all chars the same.
recall_trigger: Window size - max frequency ≤ k → valid window. Expand right, shrink left.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: In a valid window, the number of chars to replace = window_size - count_of_most_frequent_char.
approaches.key_observation: If (window_length - max_freq) > k, the window is invalid — shrink from left.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) with O(26) space. max_freq never decreases — a key optimization.

title: Minimum Window Substring
problem_summary: Find the smallest window in s that contains all characters of t (including duplicates).
recall_trigger: Two pointers: expand right until valid, then shrink left to minimize.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every substring of s for containing all of t.
approaches.key_observation: Enumerate all substrings, check if each contains all chars of t.
approaches.code_hint:
for all substrings of s:
  if contains_all(sub, t): update minimum
approaches.trade_off: Polynomial — doesn't reuse work from previous substrings.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sliding window: expand right to include all of t, then contract left to find the minimum valid window.
approaches.key_observation: Track a 'formed' counter — how many unique chars in t have their required frequency met. When formed == required, try shrinking.
approaches.code_hint:
need = Counter(t)
have = formed = 0
for r in range(n):
  if s[r] in need:
    window[s[r]] += 1
    if window[s[r]] == need[s[r]]: formed += 1
  while formed == len(need):
    update min window
    window[s[l]] -= 1
    if s[l] in need and window[s[l]] < need[s[l]]: formed -= 1
    l += 1
approaches.trade_off: O(n+m) is optimal — each char enters and leaves the window at most once.

similar_problems: Substring with Concatenation of All Words
Find All Anagrams in a String

title: Valid Parentheses
problem_summary: Determine if a string of brackets is validly matched and nested.
recall_trigger: Stack → push opens, pop on closes, check match. Stack must be empty at end.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Each closing bracket must match the most recent unmatched opening bracket — a stack naturally tracks this.
approaches.key_observation: Push opening brackets. On closing, pop and check if it matches. If stack is empty or mismatch, invalid.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time and space. Can't do better — must examine every character.

title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
problem_summary: Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted linked list.
recall_trigger: Dummy head → compare current nodes → advance the smaller one.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Compare heads of both lists, append the smaller to result, advance that pointer.
approaches.key_observation: Use a dummy node to simplify edge cases. Two pointers walk through both lists.
approaches.trade_off: O(n+m) is optimal — must examine every node.

title: Linked List Cycle
problem_summary: Determine if a linked list has a cycle.
recall_trigger: Floyd's: slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 — if they meet, cycle exists.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Use a set to track visited nodes.
approaches.key_observation: If a node is seen twice, there's a cycle.
approaches.code_hint:
seen = set()
while head:
  if head in seen: return True
  seen.add(head)
  head = head.next
approaches.trade_off: O(n) space for the set.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Two pointers at different speeds. If there's a cycle, the fast one will lap the slow one.
approaches.key_observation: Floyd's tortoise and hare. Slow moves 1, fast moves 2. They meet inside the cycle.
approaches.code_hint:
slow = fast = head
while fast and fast.next:
  slow = slow.next
  fast = fast.next.next
  if slow == fast: return True
return False
approaches.trade_off: O(1) space — no extra data structure needed.

title: Reverse Linked List
problem_summary: Reverse a singly linked list.
recall_trigger: Three pointers: prev, curr, next_temp. Reverse each link iteratively.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Walk through the list, reversing each pointer as you go.
approaches.key_observation: Need three pointers: prev (initially null), curr, and next_temp to save the next node before overwriting.
approaches.code_hint:
prev = None
while curr:
  next_temp = curr.next
  curr.next = prev
  prev = curr
  curr = next_temp
return prev
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal for any linked list traversal.

title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
problem_summary: Remove the nth node from the end of a linked list in one pass.
recall_trigger: Two pointers n apart → when fast hits end, slow is at the node to remove.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Advance fast pointer n steps ahead. Then move both until fast reaches the end. Slow is at the target.
approaches.key_observation: The gap of n between pointers means when fast reaches null, slow.next is the node to remove.
approaches.code_hint:
dummy = ListNode(0, head)
fast = slow = dummy
for _ in range(n+1): fast = fast.next
while fast:
  fast = fast.next
  slow = slow.next
slow.next = slow.next.next
return dummy.next
approaches.trade_off: Single pass, O(1) space. Optimal.

similar_problems: Delete Node in a Linked List

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
problem_summary: Merge k sorted linked lists into one sorted linked list.
recall_trigger: Min-heap of k list heads → always pop smallest, push its next.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Collect all values, sort them, build a new list.
approaches.key_observation: Flatten all lists into array, sort, reconstruct.
approaches.code_hint:
vals = []
for l in lists:
  while l: vals.append(l.val); l = l.next
vals.sort()
build linked list from vals
approaches.trade_off: Ignores the fact that lists are already sorted.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Use a min-heap to always pick the smallest among the k current heads.
approaches.key_observation: Heap maintains k elements. Pop min, push its successor. Each of N elements enters/exits heap once.
approaches.code_hint:
heap = [(l.val, i, l) for i,l in enumerate(lists) if l]
heapify(heap)
dummy = curr = ListNode()
while heap:
  val, i, node = heappop(heap)
  curr.next = node; curr = curr.next
  if node.next: heappush(heap, (node.next.val, i, node.next))
approaches.trade_off: O(N log k) — heap operations are O(log k), done N times. Optimal.

title: Reorder List
problem_summary: Reorder a linked list from L0→L1→...→Ln to L0→Ln→L1→Ln-1→...
recall_trigger: Find middle → reverse second half → merge alternately.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Split the list in half, reverse the second half, then interleave.
approaches.key_observation: Three steps: (1) find middle with slow/fast, (2) reverse second half, (3) merge two halves alternately.
approaches.code_hint:
# 1. Find middle
slow, fast = head, head.next
while fast and fast.next:
  slow = slow.next; fast = fast.next.next
# 2. Reverse second half
second = slow.next; slow.next = None
prev = None
while second: ...
# 3. Merge alternately
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal. Combines three classic linked list techniques.

title: Invert Binary Tree
problem_summary: Invert a binary tree (mirror it).
recall_trigger: Swap left and right children recursively at every node.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: At every node, swap its left and right subtrees, then recurse.
approaches.key_observation: Base case: null node. Recursive: swap children, then invert each subtree.
approaches.code_hint:
def invert(root):
  if not root: return None
  root.left, root.right = root.right, root.left
  invert(root.left)
  invert(root.right)
  return root
approaches.trade_off: O(n) visits every node exactly once. O(h) space from recursion stack.

title: Maximum Depth of Binary Tree
problem_summary: Find the maximum depth (height) of a binary tree.
recall_trigger: Depth = 1 + max(depth(left), depth(right)). Base case: null → 0.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: The depth of a tree is 1 plus the maximum depth of its subtrees.
approaches.key_observation: Recursive: return 1 + max(left_depth, right_depth). Base: null returns 0.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(h) space. Must visit every node.

title: Same Tree
problem_summary: Check if two binary trees are structurally identical with same values.
recall_trigger: Both null → true. One null → false. Same value → recurse left and right.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Recursively compare corresponding nodes of both trees.
approaches.key_observation: Base cases: both null (true), one null (false). Recursive: values match AND both subtrees match.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time — must compare every node. O(h) space for recursion.

title: Subtree of Another Tree
problem_summary: Check if a tree is a subtree of another tree.
recall_trigger: At each node, check isSameTree. If not, recurse left and right.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: For each node in the main tree, check if the subtree rooted there matches the target tree.
approaches.key_observation: Combine 'same tree' check with DFS traversal of the main tree.
approaches.trade_off: O(nm) worst case. Can optimize with hashing but rarely needed in interviews.

title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
problem_summary: Find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a BST.
recall_trigger: If both nodes < current → go left. Both > → go right. Else current is LCA.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: BST property: if both targets are smaller, LCA is in left subtree. If both larger, right. Otherwise, current node is the LCA.
approaches.key_observation: The first node where p and q diverge to different subtrees is the LCA.
approaches.code_hint:
while root:
  if p.val < root.val and q.val < root.val: root = root.left
  elif p.val > root.val and q.val > root.val: root = root.right
  else: return root
approaches.trade_off: O(h) time — follows a single root-to-node path. O(1) space iterative.

edge_cases: One node is ancestor of the other

title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
problem_summary: Return the level-order traversal of a binary tree (values grouped by level).
recall_trigger: BFS with queue → process level-by-level using queue size.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: BFS naturally processes nodes level by level. Track the level boundary using the queue size.
approaches.key_observation: At each level, process exactly len(queue) nodes (the current level's count), then their children form the next level.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time and space — every node is visited exactly once.

similar_problems: Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal

title: Validate Binary Search Tree
problem_summary: Determine if a binary tree is a valid BST.
recall_trigger: Pass valid range (min, max) down → each node must be within its ancestor's bounds.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Inorder traversal of a BST produces a sorted sequence. Check if it's sorted.
approaches.key_observation: Collect inorder values, verify strictly increasing.
approaches.trade_off: Stores all values — O(n) extra space.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Each node has a valid range. Left child must be in (-∞, parent.val), right child in (parent.val, +∞).
approaches.key_observation: Recursive: validate(node, min_val, max_val). Update bounds as you recurse.
approaches.trade_off: O(h) space from recursion — better than storing all values.

title: Kth Smallest Element in a BST
problem_summary: Find the kth smallest element in a BST.
recall_trigger: Inorder traversal of BST gives sorted order → the kth element in inorder is the answer.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: BST inorder traversal yields elements in sorted order. Stop at the kth element.
approaches.key_observation: Iterative inorder with a counter. When counter reaches k, return current value.
approaches.code_hint:
stack = []
while True:
  while root:
    stack.append(root)
    root = root.left
  root = stack.pop()
  k -= 1
  if k == 0: return root.val
  root = root.right
approaches.trade_off: O(h + k) — only traverses as far as needed. Optimal for single query.

similar_problems: Kth Largest Element in an Array

title: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder Traversal
problem_summary: Build a binary tree given its preorder and inorder traversals.
recall_trigger: Preorder first = root. Find root in inorder to split left/right subtrees. Recurse.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Preorder's first element is the root. Its position in inorder divides left and right subtrees.
approaches.key_observation: Use a hashmap for O(1) root lookups in inorder. Recurse with subarray bounds.
approaches.code_hint:
idx_map = {val:i for i,val in enumerate(inorder)}
def build(pre_start, in_start, in_end):
  if in_start > in_end: return None
  root = TreeNode(preorder[pre_start])
  mid = idx_map[root.val]
  left_size = mid - in_start
  root.left = build(pre_start+1, in_start, mid-1)
  root.right = build(pre_start+1+left_size, mid+1, in_end)
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time and space. Hashmap avoids O(n) linear search in inorder.

title: Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum
problem_summary: Find the maximum sum path in a binary tree (path can start and end at any node).
recall_trigger: At each node: max_gain = max(left, right, 0) + node.val. Update global max with left + right + node.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: At each node, the path either goes through it (left + node + right) or extends upward (node + max(left, right)).
approaches.key_observation: Return the max single-branch gain upward. Update global max with the full through-path.
approaches.code_hint:
max_sum = -inf
def gain(node):
  if not node: return 0
  left = max(gain(node.left), 0)
  right = max(gain(node.right), 0)
  max_sum = max(max_sum, left + right + node.val)
  return node.val + max(left, right)
approaches.trade_off: O(n) — single DFS pass. Must visit every node.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
problem_summary: Design an algorithm to serialize a binary tree to a string and deserialize it back.
recall_trigger: Preorder with null markers → serialize as 'val,val,#,#,...'. Deserialize with queue/index.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Preorder traversal with explicit null markers captures the exact tree structure.
approaches.key_observation: Use '#' for null nodes. Deserialize by consuming tokens in preorder — first token is root, then recursively build left then right.
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time and space. Preorder + nulls uniquely define the tree.

edge_cases: Skewed tree (all left or all right)
similar_problems: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder

title: Implement Trie (Prefix Tree)
problem_summary: Implement a trie with insert, search, and startsWith operations.
recall_trigger: Trie node = children dict + is_end flag. Walk char by char.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Each node stores a map of children (char → node) and an end-of-word flag.
approaches.key_observation: Insert: walk/create nodes for each char, mark last as end. Search: walk nodes, check end. StartsWith: walk nodes, check existence.
approaches.trade_off: O(m) per operation where m is word length. Space proportional to total chars inserted.

title: Design Add and Search Words Data Structure
problem_summary: Design a data structure that supports adding words and searching with '.' wildcard.
recall_trigger: Trie + DFS on '.' wildcard — try all children when encountering '.'.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Trie for storage. On '.', branch into all children recursively.
approaches.key_observation: Add is standard trie insert. Search uses DFS: for '.', try every child; for letters, follow the specific child.
approaches.trade_off: Worst case exponential for all-dot queries, but practical queries are fast.

title: Word Search II
problem_summary: Find all words from a dictionary that can be formed by adjacent cells in a grid.
recall_trigger: Build trie from words → DFS on grid with trie pruning → remove found words.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Build a trie from the word list. DFS from each cell, following trie branches to prune impossible paths.
approaches.key_observation: Trie prunes the search space: if no trie child matches the current cell, stop exploring. Remove found words to avoid duplicates.
approaches.trade_off: Trie + DFS is optimal for multi-word search. Pruning makes it practical.

title: Find Median from Data Stream
problem_summary: Design a data structure that supports adding numbers and finding the median efficiently.
recall_trigger: Two heaps: max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper half. Median from tops.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Split the stream into two halves. A max-heap holds the smaller half, a min-heap holds the larger half.
approaches.key_observation: Keep heaps balanced (size difference ≤ 1). Median is top of larger heap, or average of both tops.
approaches.trade_off: O(log n) insert is optimal for a streaming problem. O(1) median lookup.

similar_problems: Kth Largest Element in a Stream

title: Combination Sum
problem_summary: Find all unique combinations of candidates that sum to a target (elements can be reused).
recall_trigger: Backtrack: include current candidate (stay at same index) or skip it.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Explore all combinations recursively. At each step, either include the current number (can reuse) or move to next.
approaches.key_observation: Start index parameter prevents generating permutations (only combinations). Include same element multiple times by not advancing index.
approaches.code_hint:
def backtrack(start, target, path):
  if target == 0: result.append(path[:]); return
  for i in range(start, len(candidates)):
    if candidates[i] > target: break
    path.append(candidates[i])
    backtrack(i, target - candidates[i], path)  # i, not i+1
    path.pop()
approaches.trade_off: Exponential but unavoidable — must explore all valid combinations.

title: Number of Islands
problem_summary: Count the number of islands in a 2D grid (connected groups of '1's).
recall_trigger: Scan grid → on '1', DFS/BFS to mark entire island as visited, increment count.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Each new '1' that hasn't been visited starts a new island. Flood-fill to mark all connected '1's.
approaches.key_observation: DFS or BFS from each unvisited '1', marking cells as '0' (visited). Each trigger = one island.
approaches.trade_off: O(mn) — every cell visited at most twice. Optimal.

title: Clone Graph
problem_summary: Create a deep copy of a connected undirected graph.
recall_trigger: HashMap old→new node. DFS/BFS: clone node, then clone neighbors recursively.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Use a hashmap to track cloned nodes. For each node, create a clone and recursively clone its neighbors.
approaches.key_observation: The hashmap prevents re-cloning already-visited nodes (handles cycles).
approaches.trade_off: O(V+E) — must traverse entire graph. Hashmap handles cycles.

title: Pacific Atlantic Water Flow
problem_summary: Find cells where water can flow to both Pacific and Atlantic oceans.
recall_trigger: Reverse thinking: BFS/DFS from ocean edges inward. Intersection of both sets = answer.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Instead of checking each cell, start from ocean edges and flow inward (uphill). A cell reachable from both oceans is in the answer.
approaches.key_observation: Two separate DFS/BFS: one from Pacific edges, one from Atlantic edges. Answer = intersection.
approaches.code_hint:
pacific = set(); atlantic = set()
for i in range(m):
  dfs(i, 0, pacific)  # left edge (Pacific)
  dfs(i, n-1, atlantic)  # right edge (Atlantic)
for j in range(n):
  dfs(0, j, pacific)  # top edge (Pacific)
  dfs(m-1, j, atlantic)  # bottom edge (Atlantic)
return pacific & atlantic
approaches.trade_off: O(mn) — each cell visited at most twice. Reverse flow avoids redundant work.

title: Course Schedule
problem_summary: Determine if all courses can be finished given prerequisite pairs (detect cycle in DAG).
recall_trigger: Topological sort (Kahn's BFS) — if all nodes processed, no cycle → possible.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Model as a directed graph. If there's a cycle, it's impossible. Topological sort detects cycles.
approaches.key_observation: Kahn's algorithm: start with nodes having in-degree 0. Process them, reduce neighbors' in-degrees. If processed count < total, cycle exists.
approaches.trade_off: O(V+E) — standard graph traversal. Optimal.

title: Graph Valid Tree
problem_summary: Determine if an undirected graph is a valid tree (connected, no cycles, n-1 edges).
recall_trigger: Valid tree: exactly n-1 edges AND connected. Union-Find: if union finds same root → cycle.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: A tree with n nodes has exactly n-1 edges and is connected. Check both conditions.
approaches.key_observation: Union-Find: process each edge. If two nodes share the same root before union, it's a cycle. After all edges, check if there's exactly one component.
approaches.code_hint:
if len(edges) != n - 1: return False
parent = list(range(n))
def find(x): ...
def union(x, y):
  px, py = find(x), find(y)
  if px == py: return False  # cycle
  parent[px] = py; return True
return all(union(u,v) for u,v in edges)
approaches.trade_off: O(n·α(n)) ≈ O(n). Union-Find is optimal for dynamic connectivity.

edge_cases: No edges (n=1 is tree, n>1 not)

title: Number of Connected Components in an Undirected Graph
problem_summary: Count the number of connected components in an undirected graph.
recall_trigger: Union-Find: union all edges, count distinct roots.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Start with n components. Each successful union reduces the count by 1.
approaches.key_observation: Union-Find: initialize n components. For each edge, if roots differ, union them and decrement count.
approaches.code_hint:
parent = list(range(n))
components = n
for u, v in edges:
  pu, pv = find(u), find(v)
  if pu != pv:
    parent[pu] = pv
    components -= 1
return components
approaches.trade_off: Near O(n+E). Union-Find is the standard tool for connectivity queries.

title: Climbing Stairs
problem_summary: Count the number of distinct ways to climb n stairs (1 or 2 steps at a time).
recall_trigger: Fibonacci: ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Recursion: at each step, either take 1 or 2 stairs.
approaches.key_observation: Exponential tree of choices without memoization.
approaches.trade_off: Exponential — massive redundant computation.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Each step depends only on the previous two — classic Fibonacci pattern.
approaches.key_observation: Only need two variables, not an array. dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].
approaches.code_hint:
a, b = 1, 1
for _ in range(2, n+1):
  a, b = b, a + b
return b
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal.

title: House Robber
problem_summary: Find the maximum sum of non-adjacent elements in an array.
recall_trigger: At each house: max(rob this + skip prev, skip this). Two-variable DP.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: At each house, choose the maximum of: robbing it (+ best from 2 houses ago) or skipping it (best from 1 house ago).
approaches.key_observation: dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]). Only need last two values.
approaches.code_hint:
prev2 = prev1 = 0
for num in nums:
  temp = max(prev1, prev2 + num)
  prev2 = prev1
  prev1 = temp
return prev1
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(1) space — optimal.

title: House Robber II
problem_summary: House Robber but houses are in a circle (first and last are adjacent).
recall_trigger: Run House Robber twice: once without first house, once without last. Max of both.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: The circular constraint means you can't rob both first and last. Solve two linear subproblems.
approaches.key_observation: max(rob(nums[1:]), rob(nums[:-1])). Each is a standard House Robber.
approaches.trade_off: Two O(n) passes = O(n). Elegant reduction to solved problem.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
problem_summary: Find the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.
recall_trigger: Patience sorting: maintain tails array, binary search for insertion point.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: DP: for each element, check all previous elements for possible extensions.
approaches.key_observation: dp[i] = max(dp[j] + 1) for all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i].
approaches.trade_off: O(n²) — each element scans all predecessors.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Maintain an array of smallest tail elements for subsequences of each length. Binary search for insertion.
approaches.key_observation: tails[i] = smallest ending element of all increasing subsequences of length i+1. Use bisect_left to find where to place new element.
approaches.trade_off: O(n log n) is optimal. Binary search replaces the inner O(n) scan.

title: Longest Common Subsequence
problem_summary: Find the length of the longest common subsequence of two strings.
recall_trigger: 2D DP: if chars match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Else max(left, up).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Compare characters of both strings. If they match, extend the LCS. Otherwise, take the best from excluding one character from either string.
approaches.key_observation: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] + 1 if s1[i] == s2[j], else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
approaches.trade_off: O(mn) — can reduce space to O(min(m,n)) with rolling array.

title: Word Break
problem_summary: Determine if a string can be segmented into space-separated dictionary words.
recall_trigger: dp[i] = can s[:i] be segmented? Check all j < i where dp[j] and s[j:i] in dict.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: dp[i] is true if s[:i] can be segmented. For each position, check if any valid split point exists.
approaches.key_observation: dp[i] = any(dp[j] and s[j:i] in wordDict for j in range(i)). Start dp[0] = True (empty string).
approaches.trade_off: O(n²m) worst case where m is max word length. Can optimize with trie.

title: Coin Change
problem_summary: Find the minimum number of coins needed to make a given amount.
recall_trigger: dp[amount] = min coins. For each coin, dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Try all combinations recursively.
approaches.key_observation: For each coin denomination, either use it or don't. Exponential branching.
approaches.trade_off: Exponential without memoization.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Build up: dp[i] = minimum coins to make amount i.
approaches.key_observation: For each amount i and each coin c, dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-c] + 1).
approaches.trade_off: O(S×n) where S is amount and n is number of coin types. Optimal for this problem.

title: Decode Ways
problem_summary: Count the number of ways to decode a digit string where A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26.
recall_trigger: dp[i] = ways to decode s[:i]. Check single digit (1-9) and two digits (10-26).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: At each position, you can decode 1 digit or 2 digits (if valid). Sum the ways.
approaches.key_observation: dp[i] += dp[i-1] if s[i-1] != '0'. dp[i] += dp[i-2] if s[i-2:i] forms 10-26.
approaches.code_hint:
prev2, prev1 = 1, int(s[0] != '0')
for i in range(2, len(s)+1):
  curr = 0
  if s[i-1] != '0': curr += prev1
  two = int(s[i-2:i])
  if 10 <= two <= 26: curr += prev2
  prev2, prev1 = prev1, curr
return prev1
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(1) space — only needs previous two states.

title: Unique Paths
problem_summary: Count paths from top-left to bottom-right of an m×n grid (only right or down).
recall_trigger: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. First row and column are all 1s.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Each cell can be reached from above or from the left. Sum the ways.
approaches.key_observation: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. Base case: first row and first column are all 1.
approaches.trade_off: O(mn) time, O(n) space with rolling array. Could also use combinatorics: C(m+n-2, m-1).

title: Jump Game
problem_summary: Determine if you can reach the last index from the first, given max jump lengths.
recall_trigger: Track farthest reachable index. If current > farthest, stuck. If farthest ≥ last, done.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Greedily track the farthest index reachable. If you can reach i, you can potentially reach i + nums[i].
approaches.key_observation: farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i]). If i > farthest at any point, you're stuck.
approaches.code_hint:
farthest = 0
for i in range(n):
  if i > farthest: return False
  farthest = max(farthest, i + nums[i])
return True
approaches.trade_off: O(n) — greedy is optimal here. No need for DP.

title: Merge Intervals
problem_summary: Merge all overlapping intervals.
recall_trigger: Sort by start → if current overlaps with last merged, extend end. Else add new.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sort intervals by start time. Merge each interval with the previous if they overlap.
approaches.key_observation: After sorting, intervals overlap if current.start <= prev.end. Merge by extending prev.end = max(prev.end, current.end).
approaches.trade_off: O(n log n) from sorting — this is the bottleneck. Merging is O(n).

title: Insert Interval
problem_summary: Insert a new interval into a sorted, non-overlapping list and merge if necessary.
recall_trigger: Three phases: add all before, merge all overlapping, add all after.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Walk through intervals: add non-overlapping ones before newInterval, merge overlapping ones, add the rest.
approaches.key_observation: Interval ends before new → add it. Overlaps → merge into new. Starts after → add new, then the rest.
approaches.code_hint:
result = []; i = 0
# Add all before
while i < n and intervals[i][1] < newInterval[0]:
  result.append(intervals[i]); i += 1
# Merge overlapping
while i < n and intervals[i][0] <= newInterval[1]:
  newInterval = [min(newInterval[0],intervals[i][0]), max(newInterval[1],intervals[i][1])]
  i += 1
result.append(newInterval)
# Add remaining
result.extend(intervals[i:])
approaches.trade_off: O(n) — single pass through sorted intervals.

title: Non-overlapping Intervals
problem_summary: Find the minimum number of intervals to remove to make the rest non-overlapping.
recall_trigger: Sort by end → greedily keep intervals that end earliest (maximize non-overlapping count).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sort by end time. Always keep the interval that ends earliest (gives most room for future intervals).
approaches.key_observation: Count non-overlapping intervals (greedy selection). Answer = total - count.
approaches.code_hint:
intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[1])
count = 0; end = -inf
for s, e in intervals:
  if s >= end:
    count += 1
    end = e
return len(intervals) - count
approaches.trade_off: O(n log n) sorting dominates. Greedy is optimal for interval scheduling.

title: Meeting Rooms
problem_summary: Determine if a person can attend all meetings (no two meetings overlap).
recall_trigger: Sort by start → if any meeting starts before the previous one ends, conflict.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sort by start time. If any consecutive meetings overlap, return false.
approaches.key_observation: After sorting, overlap exists if intervals[i].start < intervals[i-1].end.
approaches.code_hint:
intervals.sort()
for i in range(1, len(intervals)):
  if intervals[i][0] < intervals[i-1][1]: return False
return True
approaches.trade_off: O(n log n) from sorting. Linear scan after.

title: Meeting Rooms II
problem_summary: Find the minimum number of conference rooms required.
recall_trigger: Sort by start → min-heap tracks end times. If earliest end ≤ start, reuse that room.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Sort meetings by start time. Use a min-heap to track the earliest ending meeting.
approaches.key_observation: If the earliest ending room is free (end ≤ current start), reuse it (pop and push). Otherwise, allocate a new room (just push).
approaches.trade_off: O(n log n) — sorting + heap operations. Optimal.

title: Reverse Bits
problem_summary: Reverse the bits of a 32-bit unsigned integer.
recall_trigger: Extract LSB of n, shift result left, add bit. Repeat 32 times.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Process each bit from right to left. Shift result left and add each extracted bit.
approaches.key_observation: result = (result << 1) | (n & 1). Then n >>= 1. Repeat 32 times.
approaches.code_hint:
result = 0
for _ in range(32):
  result = (result << 1) | (n & 1)
  n >>= 1
return result
approaches.trade_off: O(1) — fixed 32 iterations. Optimal.

title: Number of 1 Bits
problem_summary: Count the number of set bits (1s) in a binary representation.
recall_trigger: Brian Kernighan's trick: n & (n-1) removes the lowest set bit. Count removals.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: n & (n-1) turns off the lowest set bit. Count how many times until n becomes 0.
approaches.key_observation: Brian Kernighan's trick — only iterates as many times as there are set bits, not 32.
approaches.code_hint:
count = 0
while n:
  n &= n - 1
  count += 1
return count
approaches.trade_off: O(k) where k is number of set bits. More efficient than checking all 32 bits.

title: Counting Bits
problem_summary: For every number 0 to n, count the number of 1 bits.
recall_trigger: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1). Right-shift reuses already-computed counts.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: The number of set bits in i is the same as i>>1 (drop last bit) plus the last bit itself.
approaches.key_observation: dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1). Each number reuses the result of a smaller number.
approaches.code_hint:
dp = [0] * (n + 1)
for i in range(1, n+1):
  dp[i] = dp[i >> 1] + (i & 1)
return dp
approaches.trade_off: O(n) — one pass, constant work per number.

title: Missing Number
problem_summary: Find the one missing number in [0, n] given n numbers.
recall_trigger: XOR all indices and values — duplicates cancel, missing number remains. Or: sum formula.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: XOR is self-inverse: a ^ a = 0. XOR all numbers with all indices — the missing one won't cancel.
approaches.key_observation: result = n (start with n since indices go 0..n-1). XOR each index and value.
approaches.code_hint:
result = len(nums)
for i, num in enumerate(nums):
  result ^= i ^ num
return result
approaches.trade_off: O(n) time, O(1) space. Alternative: sum(0..n) - sum(nums).

title: Sum of Two Integers
problem_summary: Add two integers without using + or - operators.
recall_trigger: XOR = sum without carry. AND << 1 = carry. Repeat until carry is 0.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: XOR gives the sum ignoring carries. AND gives the carry positions. Shift carry left and repeat.
approaches.key_observation: sum = a ^ b (add without carry). carry = (a & b) << 1. Repeat with sum and carry until carry is 0.
approaches.code_hint:
while b != 0:
  carry = (a & b) << 1
  a = a ^ b
  b = carry
return a
approaches.trade_off: O(1) — at most 32 iterations for 32-bit integers.

title: Spiral Matrix
problem_summary: Return all elements of a matrix in spiral order.
recall_trigger: Four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right. Traverse → shrink → repeat.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Maintain four boundaries. Traverse each edge of the current rectangle, then shrink inward.
approaches.key_observation: Process: right along top row → down along right col → left along bottom row → up along left col. Shrink boundaries after each.
approaches.trade_off: O(mn) — must visit every element. Boundary checks prevent double-counting.

title: Rotate Image
problem_summary: Rotate an n×n matrix 90° clockwise in-place.
recall_trigger: Transpose (swap rows/cols) → then reverse each row.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: A 90° clockwise rotation = transpose + reverse each row.
approaches.key_observation: Transpose: swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]. Then reverse each row in-place.
approaches.trade_off: O(n²) in-place. Must touch every element.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
problem_summary: If an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0. Do it in-place.
recall_trigger: Use first row/col as markers. Scan → mark → fill → handle first row/col separately.

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Track which rows and columns contain zeros using extra sets.
approaches.key_observation: Two sets: zero_rows and zero_cols. Second pass sets elements to 0.
approaches.trade_off: O(m+n) space for the sets.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Use the first row and first column as markers instead of extra space.
approaches.key_observation: First pass: mark zeros in first row/col. Handle first row/col separately with a flag. Second pass: fill based on markers.
approaches.trade_off: O(1) space — uses the matrix itself as storage.

title: Longest Palindromic Substring
problem_summary: Find the longest palindromic substring in a string.
recall_trigger: Expand around each center (both odd and even length palindromes).

approaches.label: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check every substring for palindrome property.
approaches.key_observation: O(n²) substrings, each check is O(n).
approaches.code_hint:
for all substrings:
  if is_palindrome(sub): update longest
approaches.trade_off: Cubic — redundant palindrome checks.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Every palindrome has a center. Expand outward from each possible center.
approaches.key_observation: n centers for odd-length, n-1 for even-length. Expand while characters match.
approaches.code_hint:
def expand(l, r):
  while l>=0 and r<n and s[l]==s[r]: l-=1; r+=1
  return s[l+1:r]

best = ''
for i in range(n):
  odd = expand(i, i)
  even = expand(i, i+1)
  best = max(best, odd, even, key=len)
approaches.trade_off: O(n²) time, O(1) space. Manacher's gives O(n) but rarely expected in interviews.

title: Palindromic Substrings
problem_summary: Count the total number of palindromic substrings.
recall_trigger: Expand around each center → count all valid expansions (same as LPS but count instead of track).

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Same as Longest Palindromic Substring, but count every valid expansion instead of tracking the longest.
approaches.key_observation: Each successful expansion step is one more palindromic substring.
approaches.trade_off: O(n²) — same approach as LPS. Each expansion is O(n) worst case.

title: Alien Dictionary
problem_summary: Given a sorted list of words in an alien language, determine the character ordering.
recall_trigger: Compare adjacent words → extract char ordering edges → topological sort.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: Adjacent words in sorted order reveal character precedence. Build a graph, then topological sort.
approaches.key_observation: Compare word[i] and word[i+1] char by char. First difference gives an edge: char_a → char_b. Topological sort gives the order.
approaches.trade_off: O(C) where C is total characters. Topological sort is O(V+E).

title: Word Search
problem_summary: Find if a word exists in a grid by connecting adjacent cells (each cell used once).
recall_trigger: DFS from each cell matching word[0] → mark visited → backtrack on failure.

approaches.label: Optimal
approaches.intuition: DFS/Backtracking: try to match the word starting from each cell, exploring adjacent cells.
approaches.key_observation: Mark cells as visited during exploration, unmark on backtrack. Prune when character doesn't match.
approaches.code_hint:
def dfs(i, j, k):
  if k == len(word): return True
  if out_of_bounds or board[i][j] != word[k]: return False
  board[i][j] = '#'  # mark visited
  found = any(dfs(ni,nj,k+1) for ni,nj in neighbors(i,j))
  board[i][j] = word[k]  # restore
  return found
approaches.trade_off: O(mn × 3^L) — 3 directions (not revisiting previous cell). Exponential but pruned.
